sentences.text: The three older brothers, Phillip, Ben, and Bernie, were taken away to Hungarian labor camps, [NON-ENGLISH].
Father, mother, my brother Bill, Sam, and I and my sister were there.
In addition, there were my cousin and my uncles and aunts from the same village, Uncle Yosef, his wife, and their children.
And sadly, only one is alive today, and she's in Israel.
She was trapped in Russia.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we were joined by a lot of other cousins and other family, but most of all, all my friends, basically the whole village.
Jewish people were there, and also the surrounding villages.
And then they started bringing from the other side of the city-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews into-- into the ghetto.
Yes, but-- but we weren't there that long, because the next thing we knew, they brought in cattle cars.
It was about three weeks, third week, something like that.
Time runs away from you when-- when-- when you're faced with such grief, and so you don't know days, you don't know nights a lot of times.
And next thing we knew, in the morning they pulled in cattle cars.
And they ordered us to leave everything and get into the cattle car.
Well, you know, at first we didn't want to leave everything, just like we didn't want to leave everything in our home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was no room in the cattle car, really.
And they jammed in somewhere between 80 and 100 people in the cattle car.
It was a small car.

As a matter of fact, when I went to the US Museum, Holocaust Museum in Washington, my daughter finally pulled me away after half an hour-- I kept on looking and looking and looking to that cattle car that they brought in as a sample, because I couldn't figure out how in the world they managed to cram so many of us in there.
It's true we couldn't move.
We couldn't turn.
And you could only stand up.
And hell broke loose after half an hour, because there were people who-- there were teeny little babies.
They might have been a week, two week, three weeks old.
Those babies were crying.
They wanted to be fed, and in those days there were no bottles.
So obviously these women were very religious, and they didn't want to be exposing themselves to people around them until there was no choice.
And we tried very desperately to turn around, not to embarrass these women, but we couldn't.
There was no room.
And if that wasn't bad enough, the little boys, after two, three hours, had to make wee-wee, and they started crying.
And the little girls, the same thing.
So they started wee-weeing on the next person or in their pants.
And then the next thing, the elderly couldn't stand on their feet.
They started collapsing.
So there was shoving and screaming and crying to try to protect that nobody should step on their heads.
And this went on and on.

And the thing that sticks most in my mind about that ride, suddenly my father-- may he rest in peace-- was a very, very strict man-- he never, never asked me anything twice-- suddenly started pleading with me in Yiddish, Yankele, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
And I looked at him.
I was no different.
I was 15 and 1/2 years old.
I was a typical wise guy at 15.
I said, well, my father's over the hill already.
He's already over 40 years old.

And meanwhile, our mother, who never, ever asked-- had to ask us for anything because we used to worship the ground she walked on, suddenly she looks at me, and she pleaded, Yankele, I want you should listen to Father.
And you know, it was so difficult.
Here I was-- I thought of myself as a growing young man, and why would my father suddenly plead with me I should live?
Here is-- I thought he was over the hill already, you know.
He was 40 years old.
But he must have had a feeling that something was not right, that where we are going is not what they promised us.

Because the Germans, the SS men, promised us that they were going to take us away from these local Jew-hating anti-Semites They're going to relocate us in Germany, where we're going to have respectable jobs and homes, and we're going to be valuable citizens in order to produce, you know, with our skill.
Suddenly we are in this cattle car.
And the Bedlam was there.
And my father's just pleading with me.
He never, ever asked me for anything, because he was very strict.
And of course, later on I realized what he meant by it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He felt that he was not going to make it, that he wanted his children to make it.
And it stayed with me all through the camps.
I said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- don't talk foolishness.
Because I thought it was-- why is he doing it?
The biggest thing that troubled me, why is he doing-- why is he asking me that question?
Yes, I-- I-- I was hoping.
I didn't believe it, necessarily, but I was hoping that they were right, because I just couldn't take any more of the abuse that we endured in the-- in the village over there by these Hitler Youth.
It was-- it was awful to see your best friend suddenly become your worst enemies.
That was a very, very difficult, very painful thing.
And as time went on-- this lasted not a day or two days.
This lasted like four or five days.
We were basically out of our faculties.
We were out of our minds by the time we arrived.
And when the door was opened up from the outside, when we arrived at the destination, which turned out to be Birkenau, and suddenly we started hearing the screams, Jude raus-- Jew out.
It was chilling.
It was unbelievable.
And suddenly they started kicking us and beating us.
And before I knew it, I lost all my family.
I didn't-- suddenly didn't find no one, because they separated us the minute we got off, separated men, women, children, babies, elderly, crippled.
The-- the children and the crippled and the elderly-- and when you say elderly, I'm talking about anybody over 40, 35, 40 years old.
Suddenly, I couldn't find my brothers, couldn't find my sister, couldn't find Mother or Father.
And I saw a skinny man in a striped uniform walking and-- and dragging his feet.
He was very skinny.
He was a muselmann, and he was blumbering, [SPEAKING GERMAN], you're out.
You're 18 years old.
And I looked at him.
Are you crazy?
What is happening here?
I thought that maybe this is not real.
This is just a nightmare.
But when I found myself in front of the SS man, that he had his hands folded, and he looked at me, and he said in German, how old are you?
I said 18, even though I was only 15 and 1/2, because anybody that day that wasn't 18 was sent straight to the gas chambers.
And to this day-- and I verified it when I went back last year, that yes, I was right, that anybody that was sent straight and bearing right, that's where the gas chamber was.
And I remember seeing from the back my father with his hat.
I couldn't believe it, because at that time, you know, everything went so fast.
I was sent to the left, and I lost everybody.
I didn't see anybody in my family.
Next thing, I found myself in Birkenau.
I was ordered to take my clothes off.
You know, I had my best suit on.
[LAUGHS] I didn't want to.
And somebody was there, whipped me up real good.
And so I took my clothes off.
And the next thing I knew, they tore my hair off.
And then I stood in line to be tattooed.
You know, there was guy there with a burning thing.
He had the needle there.
He started tattooing a number on me.
And when he got finished, I looked around, and I saw my brother, Sam, walking.
And typical human nature, I got up and I wanted to go and hug him.
And, you know-- and they beat the hell out of me.
Because they told me I was no longer part of the human race.
I was only a number.
I was a Jew.
So I fell in line with my group, and they ordered me to go to another area, where I was given a striped uniform and wooden clogs-- no underwear, nothing.
And it was still very, very cold in the Silesian mountains, and I was freezing, so tried to move around to get a little blood flowing.
And they started marching us out from there.
And as they marched us past the women's section, I heard cries-- Yankele!
Yankele!
I recognized the voice, but I looked over, and, you know, I saw probably 100 people with handkerchief and a striped frock over them.
I didn't know whether they were women or men.
And as I came closer, it turned out it was the voice of Mother and my sister.
So, again by nature, I forgot the beating I got.
And I wanted to go and just to touch her and just to kiss her if I can.
But again, the kapo and the SS men just almost beat me to death.
In fact, they gave me a good, swift kick in my shoulder that sometimes it still hurts.
From there, they marched me to the main camp of Auschwitz.
And the following day, they put me on trucks to take me to Auschwitz III, what is called Buna or Buna Werke.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The camp over there is-- is-- is beyond human description.
And I'll give you a good example.
Elie Wiesel, when he got his honorary doctorate at Emory, I picked him up-- he and I are very close.
He and I went to all the camps together.
We were liberated together.
And we talked in Yiddish.
And I asked him, when I picked him up at 5:00 in the morning, I said Elie, I heard you talk.
I've read your book.
How comes you have never, never talked about one hour, one day that we endured in Buna?
And he looks at me, and he says to me in Yiddish, Yankele, do you believe you survived one hour?
And I just shut up.
I couldn't say nothing.
But every so often, I get together with him, and I want to talk about it, because what we experienced in that camp is beyond human understanding.
It wasn't just the beatings.
It wasn't just the hunger.
It wasn't just the hard work.
It's the constant abuse.
And you never know whether you were going to get food.
If you were lucky, you got once a day bread, or once every second day bread.
If you were lucky, you got a bowl of soup.
And the only possession you had was the little bowl with a spoon.
And if you lost that, you lost your life.
It was given to us when we got there.
And that's it.
We had a string attached.
We had no belts.
We had a string attached, and that's the only thing that kept our pants up, because obviously we lost weight.
We were very skinny.
But what made it so difficult that-- I can only tell you that only thing that kept us alive was the hard labor-- really.
It was so hard that it took our minds off our hunger pains, our beating pains, our disease pains.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every day people were dying.
People were being taken to the crematorium.
And every day, they brought in 1,500 people, 2,000 people-- healthy ones.
And I think the average lifespan might have been a month, maybe five weeks.
I found out, actually, because the smell was so horrible when we arrived in Birkenau that one of the inmates was telling us that they're burning up people here.
They're gassing them, burning them.
You didn't want to believe it, but you couldn't help it because the smell-- the stench was awful.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this wasn't only time.
Several other times.
And also the drivers used to brag-- the German drivers used to brag, we bring healthy ones, and we take these people to be burned.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The difficult part to explain, for me, is probably how fellow humans were so vicious to us.
It was so unbelievable.
We had there Hungarian guards.
We had there fascist guards.
We had there Ukrainians.
We had there Slovaks.
We had Polish Nazi guards there.
But they were so vicious, so beyond human comprehension-- just for kicks.
Oh, there are too many to describe.
One time, I remember-- you know, we were divided into barracks and work-- work details.
I worked most of the time in a-- in-- in a electric warehouse.
Except with the bombing, and we had to go repair the streets and railroad cars and so forth.
And we were making walls to protect in case there's a-- there's a air raid, that the whole warehouse shouldn't burn down.
And these blocks were big, and we had to lift them up and-- and stack them up and, you know, build the walls.
But we also carried heavy equipment.
And they had stored in there anti-aircraft gun-- the-- the-- the-- what do you call-- the-- the-- the gauges that used to-- they used to shoot with these guns the Allied planes.
So we knocked on the quicksilver, which ruined their equipment.
As a result, they couldn't shoot our planes down, the Allied planes down.
But they found out, so they used to come in and just take two or three guys every day and shoot them or torture, cut an arm off.
Another time, a group of SS men came into our section there, where our barrack was, and ordered two boys to pick up a boy and throw him up.
And they used to put the bayonet on the gun and see how deep the bayonet would go into the boy.
Or another group used to come in, and they bet whether he's going to shoot the left or right eye out of a young boy.
And there was also a-- similar to Ilse Koch.
She used to come in and make us stand out in the cold winter so she could check the tattoos, what color skin we have so she would pick what color she wanted to make lamp shades out of.
And the SS men used to order the arm to be whacked off.
It's very difficult to describe.
But what is more even difficult is that everybody else of the Germans and the civilians just stood by watching, didn't care-- I mean, didn't care at all.
That was even more painful than the vicious things that these guards used to do.
The only time I experienced that was when they evacuated us from the camp, and one of the Hungarian guards turned to the other Hungarian guards, and he says, we are going to be captured anyway.
The Germans are going to lose the war.
And he put his gun to his mouth, and he blew his brains out.
That's the only time, really, the entire time that I saw any emotion that-- that they might lose the war, and they should stop doing it.
Other than that, no.
[LAUGHS] That's a-- That's-- that's something that neither Elie Wiesel or I or any one of us survivors have really been able to figure out.
I don't know.
I guess the-- the main thing that was in my mind-- my commitment to Father, Mother that no matter what, I'm going to stay alive.
And also you had to be very strong to try and help your fellow man.
Because too many people were ready to give up.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Many times, you woke up and person next to you was dead.
And-- and somehow if-- if-- if they were having any breathing in them, any warmth, you kept on shaking and making sure-- please, please, please, come on.
Wake up!
Wake up!
Wake up!
Because we got very little sleep over there for many reasons.
Number one, you know, we had no mattresses.
We had no straw.
We had these rough boards.
And we didn't have-- we couldn't lay flat, because we had only this much room.
And most of all, you had to guard your bread, because if people thought that you were dead, they took your bread away from you or took your pants off of you.
So you had stay up all night.
When I tell people now that if I'm lucky I get two, three hours sleep a night, they can't believe it.
But that's true.
I feel very fortunate if I can get that much sleep.
That camp has-- has taught me-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] suffering beyond any human understanding.
I don't think humans can ever, ever understand the suffering that we went through there.
And this went on and on for about eight months till the Russians were coming close, and they started evacuating these camps.
And I remember coming back-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] late that day.
As a matter of fact, let me go back a little bit.
The bombings were going on day after day.
And, of course, we were praying and begging the Almighty, please have the bomb hit me already.
Take me out of my misery.
But yet you didn't want to give up on life.
And one time, I remember, that it's broad daylight.
It was about noon.
We didn't know whether it was Monday, Saturday, a holiday, or anything, because you had no comprehension what day it was or what-- what went on.
And as the planes arrived, the German SS men all went into the shelters.
And we were there outside.
And suddenly a boy run-- jumps, runs on top of a pile of dirt, and he starts praying.
It would happen to have been Rosh Hashanah.
And we were all around him and praying.
We had no siddur.
We had no mahzor.
We had no prayer books.
But we prayed there.
And the bombing lasted for about two, three hours.
[LAUGHS] And we managed to get some praying in.
And after-- right after that, we were clearing out some of the rubbles after the bombing-- as a matter of fact, in the building that I worked in.
And as I was pounding away-- I couldn't move the rubble.
And it turned out, when we cleared the way, it was a bomb.
And it was a long, long bomb.
So they made us tie ropes around it.
And we were dragging it out.
And just as we loaded it into one of the-- where a bomb crate was, where a bomb fell, it went off.
If it would have gone off one second earlier, I wouldn't have been here to talk about it.
But-- those-- those bombings gave us a little reprieve.
[LAUGHS] Because we didn't have to work so hard at that time.
We were just able to stand outside, and hopefully it was raining or whatever.
Then we could relax a little bit.
No.
We worked in packaging and-- and carrying those gauges and storing them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mostly we did only heavy manual labor, nothing else.
Oh yes.
We-- there were a lot of them, as a matter of fact.
I know for a fact that we sabotaged a railroad tracks.
Well, one of the gangs that worked with me that was in our barrack, they worked on repairing the railroad tracks.
And just as the SS men went to whistle and line up up front, he managed-- he-- he thought he saw them tied down, but instead of that, he managed to push the rail onto the side.
And of course, when the train came, the whole train was derailed.
Oh, it was a lot of punishment-- a lot of punishment.
But-- but we-- we had constant punishment.
The punishment, to us, was just a-- a routine thing.
They-- they-- they had all kinds of excuses to punish us.
If the weather was no good, they punished us.
If the weather was too warm, they punished us.
If the weather was too cold, they punished us.
They took their frustrations out on us.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course, they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they must have been miserable people at home, because they certainly got their pleasures out of making our lives miserable.
Not only was I not able to get any information about my family, I-- I didn't-- [SIGHS]-- I didn't know whether I was alive.

But finally, about the third or fourth month I was there, as I was being marched from the appellplatz, from the main section, to watch as they were hanging some people-- they put up hanging parades-- I looked over, and I saw my brother Sam.
He was in the same camp.
We were not allowed to get together, but we somehow or other managed to see each other.
We did everything possible to at least see each other once a week.
No, but I was able to throw him a kiss or wave at him and sometimes say [NON-ENGLISH], how are you?
But it wasn't too often that we got the chance, because he was way in the other end of the camp.
And all we did is encourage each other.
Just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] please stay alive.
Are you OK?
Are you all right?
As a matter of fact, one time a truck of-- a full truck with tomatoes was passing the road, and then it a little bump, and some tomatoes fell over.
Of course, we dove for it.
And I picked up five little, teeny tomatoes, green tomatoes.
And I carried them inside my jacket for about three or four days till I found my brother Sam to give it to him.
Because I didn't eat tomatoes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We did a lot of those kind of things.
I think it was very important for me to know that somebody else in my family's alive.
Because I had no idea where anybody else is.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
We became friends with everybody-- anybody and everybody.
We-- we-- we really cared for each other.
We did everything possible to try and encourage each other.
If somebody wanted to hang themselves because they couldn't take it any more-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we somehow talked 'em out of it.
If somebody, for instance, said, I'm giving up, I can't do it any more, we somehow talked them out of it.
We maybe shared a little piece of bread.
The bread was mostly made out of sawdust, but somehow we put a little piece of bread in his mouth.
We did all kinds of those kinds of things.
If somebody was hit real bad and they had a wound, we somehow managed to put on little thing, maybe tore off part of our white jacket, you know, striped jacket, to keep it on there.
But somehow we helped each other.
We encouraged each other with whatever we had to keep on going, not to give up.
What was the worst aspect?
Oh, there are too many.
